title: 2025/10/08 - GA Subcommittee B
displayTitle: 2025-10-08 - Governance and Administration Committee - Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Bill [Subcommittee B]
description: Wednesday, 8th October 2025 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee B Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence: New Plymouth District Council Neil Holdom - Mayor Tararua District Council Peter Wimsett - Chief Advisor Citizens Advice Bureau Kerry Dalton - CEO Tourism Industry Aotearoa Bruce Bassett - Chief Advisor Hamilton City Council Paula Southgate, Mayor Nelson Tasman Climate Forum David Bartle - Member New Zealand Taxpayers' Union James Ross - Head of Policy and Legislative Affairs Ray Deacon - Economist Jordan Williams - Executive Director NZ Airports Association Billie Moore - Chief Executive Phil Stevens Theo van de Klundert Ashburton District Council Liz McMillian - Deputy Mayor Hamish Riach - Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: Local Government (System Improvements) Amendment Bill. New Plymouth District Council. Neil Holdom - Mayor

chunks.text: You know, when there's a minute to run, but you might like to leave some time for questions. Otherwise, all yours, Great. Uh, thank you Te Nui. Hi everyone. I'm Neil. I'll be one of your mayors today. Um, thank you for the opportunity to speak. Um, we'll take our submission as read. I think everybody agrees. We want councils and government to be as efficient as possible. Um, the journey to efficiency at scale is long and continuous.

Take a look around crumbling hospitals, schools, state highways, police stations, and defense assets, but this regulation is about council. So let's consider our collective scale and impact. In the 20 23 24 financial year, new Zealand's local councils had combined operating expenditure of around 18.1 billion in total assets of approximately 217 billion, uh, with total revenue also reported around 17.8 billion.

Um, council debt at that stage moved from 31 billion to 35 billion by 2024, uh, representing about 200 to 210% of their income over on average. Um, and a significant increase since the year 2000. In terms of debt, um, you may see local government is small, but our sector is huge and you need to get this regulation right.

The key objectives have gotta be to deliver infrastructure and services which meet local expectations, support growth in housing and the economy efficiently protect and enhance publicly owned wealth. It is my view if you want to do these things credibly, you should reflect on examples of successful regulation in New Zealand and what can be learned from them.

Lines, companies in New Zealand were first significantly regulated as monopolies under the Commerce Act in 1986, which involved a special regime for large electricity lines businesses in its part four, later amended to part four A in 2001. This framework was established to monitor the prices revenue quality to ensure fair competition and consumer benefits were the first regulatory decisions on thresholds issued.

Seven years later, in 2003, subsequent changes have been made to the Commerce Act to ship the current, to the current default price path regime, which allows for companies to apply for customized price, price paths with our ramping up investment to meet growing demand. Um, three decades of carefully managed and modified economic regulation, which has worked for New Zealand.

Lions companies are investing appropriately on their networks to meet demand, and they're efficient. So what can we learn from this legislation? Before government can efficiently and effectively regulate, um, pricing in local government, they need to undertake detailed benchmarking to determine efficient prices. Should rural Southland be expected to deliver the same efficiency factor as Auckland?

Average rates vary from around $1,500 a year in some parts of the country to 5,000 a year, depending on where you live. If you applied a blanket cap of say, CPI plus growth, plus 2%, should a council, which has kept rates well below average, be restricted from investing in projects which support their communities.

I developed a benchmark for councils and received data from around 30 of the councils, um, through their long-term plans. I can send you a link to the data and the portal and the templates that we developed with a view that local government does need to benchmark itself on the journey to efficiency. We need to standardize the way councils report their core financial information for the 27 28 long-term plans.

We need to consolidate all council's data as part of our national data strategy, capturing 10 to 30 year financial forecast for roads and transport, water, wastewater, stormwater, waste, minimization and management parks and public spaces, museums, libraries, art galleries, parks, venues, public spaces, economic development and community development.

We need to digitize the information produced in local government, analyze it, and carefully regulated over time. The risk of getting it wrong would be further decline in the public asset base and the potential to stifle housing growth. Nobody wants their local assets run down, and very few people want to stifle housing growth.

So let's be pragmatic. Ask yourself if you really have enough information to confidently regulate the activities of all councils across New Zealand in a way that won't undermine any of your other policy objectives for economic growth in housing. I don't think you do, and I believe you should be focused on benchmarking through information disclosure first to inform better decisions.

Hold off on price caps until you have a clear grasp of this multi-billion dollar sector. Um, down in the detail of the bill, um, we have a perpetual investment fund. It's, um, projected, uh, protected by a super majority clause in our standing orders that requires 75% of council to vote to open up the capital base.

You guys can't be messing with our super majority requirement through these standardized standing orders. We need the ability to protect that asset, um, because ultimately this, if it's, if it drops out, it can be an opportunist council that can deliver a 50% majority could crack into our, uh, the capital base of our perpetual investment fund.

Happy to take any questions. Okay. Thank you very much. I'm just, uh, looking around the room to see stunned. They, you have, uh, made an excellent submission. Thank you. I think that is, uh, really clear and I appreciate you taking the time to join us today.

Um, Neil, thank you very much. Cool. And look, the one other thing if you wanted, I can send you, I, I circulated across the sector a draft benchmark template. Um, we could provide that to the committee and we can also provide a, um, business information portal where you could access, because what I suggest is that the DIA looks to expand on the benchmarking they've done with something far more specific that would support better regulatory outcomes.

So happy to send that through if you're interested. Thank you. And just in the last 30 seconds, can I ask one quick, one of the suggestions we had was around core services, and rather than having a list having priorities, how would that work for You? I think that the, it depends on whether you're gonna apply a price path, um, is, is the key thing.

And the definitions need to be really clear because otherwise we are just gonna end up funding lawyers trying to determine, um, you know, what what is defined as core. Okay. Hey, thank you very much once again for your time today. We are gonna move to our next submission, which will be from the tourism industry.

Good. Um, pika, is it, uh, Bruce. Bruce, I apologize. Uh, it is, sorry I'm reading the wrong line. Um, Bruce, thank you for, uh, joining us today in person. Um, there's 10 minutes. I'll let you know when there's a minute to run. Otherwise, the, um, the floor is all yours. Cool. Um, thank you so much, uh, appreciate the opportunity for speaking to our submission, uh, this morning.

Uh, name's, uh, Bruce Bassett. I'm, uh, chief at Pfizer at Tourism Industry. Aurora, uh, my chief executive, um, Rebecca Ingram, uh, was unable to attend, so she passes on her apologies. Uh, and, um, yeah, she, you know, um, you know, this is very, um, um, so, so, um, TAA is a and is tope association, the tourism industry.

We've got 1200 or so, uh, uh, business members. So we speak very broadly for all types of tourism businesses. Uh, and so, um, and there support a number of the other associations that your aspects. Um, so our, our, just to run through some of the key points from our submission, um, and, you know, firstly, um, um, our interpretation of the bill is that by focusing on the core activities and the way that is being done will limit the ability of councils to, um, to do the work that they're currently doing with, uh, business, you know,

economic development and with tourism within that bundle. Um, and, you know, to explore that a little further, um, we very much look at tourism as a system, uh, and it a it's a great massive people moving around the country and domestic living internationally, and they, they come and go. They go pass through a whole bunch of different hands.

Uh, and there are, there are three key, you know, sort of sectors that are, uh, have part to play the tourism industry itself, our multitude of member businesses and the like. They provide services and products to, to visitors, uh, and they're really good at it. Um, so they, they do that well. And, um, great, great experiences for our visitors.

Uh, central government's got a whole range of roles to play with. Its policy settings. It supports tourism and marketing. Uh, it, it sets rules of the game and it runs some programs to support, um, parts of, of the industry itself. So really important in that respect. Uh, the local government role, uh, is equally important, if not more and important.

Uh, the local government looks after the place and tourism takes place at the place. Um, so how local government sort of runs its, uh, its location and its facilities, amenities, uh, and so forth helps, um, you know, supports events, uh, operates this destination management plans invests in things like, you know, um, uh, local regional airports, uh, conference facilities and like, they're just vitally important parts of the fabric of the tourism industry.

Uh, and you know, to swing back to my first comment, we see that the bill has worded, uh, really, uh, creates a, an enormous amount of risk for the way the tourism industry functions from the local government perspective. Uh, and, uh, the, the committee, you know, really ask you to be, be be aware of that, uh, consequential, um, aspect of the bill in relation to the tourism industry and those local government functions.

Um, the government has set a growth agenda, um, and it's articulated in tourism through the tourism growth agenda that the Minister of Tourism and Hospitality, um, Louise Abston, uh, introduced, uh, a few months ago. Uh, and there's a lot of work going on with that, um, process at the moment, and it's looking at a whole range of things that are relevant to the work of your workers, um, of the committee in that, how the tourism system works, uh, what are the structures, what are the funding mechanisms, what roles get best done where, uh, and so forth.

So the government's goal is to double, uh, tourism exports by, uh, 2034. Um, tourism industry very much supports that. Uh, but we, we do believe, and the minister believes that there was needs to be work done on the supply side of the industry, make sure we've got the capacity and the quality, uh, to achieve the goal.

So that's, you know, again, a, a portion of that falls, falls to local government to play a, an active, uh, role in being able to do so. Um, so you know, that, that, that's kind of why this is important to us as a, as an industry association and to the industry more broadly.

Um, and, um, when we picked into the wording of the bill and the, uh, the, the, the paper going into it, um, we started seeing, um, a, a mismatch between what was intended perhaps and what was actually reflected in the bill, uh, as we read it.

Uh, the problem with the, the bill is that as it's worded, it allows economic growth and development, but only for the purposes of local infrastructure, local public services, performance of regulatory functions. So within that, we don't see any way that, um, the purpose of local government will be enabled to do the tourism functions.

Uh, and that's the, the another our, our, our concern. Um, we sort of, uh, just reinforcing when we looked at the, um, uh, um, the cabinet paper and how the minister articulated what he was seeking to achieve from the, from the bill, uh, it, it, it actually wasn't there.

It was about an opening economic growth and development and doing that appropriately. So we think, um, in, in our submission, we suggest different wording that actually incorporates, uh, economic growth and develop economic development, um, within, uh, that section, uh, 10 be itself. Uh, and that would more directly enable, uh, the tourism functions to be undertaken.

So that's what we got with Bill. We suggest some wording, uh, changes, uh, and, uh, we really, really, um, for the committee to, to take that seriously. 'cause the consequences for the tourism industry, uh, had a whole bunch of local government activities, um, be extremely serious, um, of our local authorities.

Uh, nearly all of them, uh, if not all of them could do tourism s at the moment. Some of them do really important large scale, um, things, and they create a lot of benefit for communities from doing those, whether it's hosting events, festivals, and the like, that really, um, you know, generate, um, some vibe.

Um, you know, and they support local businesses. You know, I, we start the example of TPO in this, uh, submission where, um, a really large proportion of the, uh, food and beverage places wouldn't exist without tourism. A large proportion of their retail activities wouldn't, uh, exist without tourism. Uh, local government's got a really important roll through its seasonality strategies and attracting events in the, like, as a lever for, for change, you know, for improving, um, you know, um, a whole bunch of outcomes for communities.

So I'll leave it at that and really happy to take any questions. Okay. Uh, it looks like we've got a few, very quickly at the moment, tourism, uh, councils can still put money into economic growth and development, um, but under this legislation only by the provision of good quality infrastructure, public services and, and tourism's view, are you saying would be that, say promoting the region, um, wouldn't fit into the good quality public infrastructure, public services, therefore it can't, can't be done.

Um, it, that, that would be our interpretation of it. Yes. Um, but you'd Like to be able to put money into what promotion of the area, say a visit wire campaign Or something? Absolutely. You know, and then, and then do the other thing. It's not just promotion. It's not just marketing. It's about, about investing the fabric of the place so that you have a, you know, nice urban, you know, nice, uh, you know, quality environment for people to participate in.

And that's where the mixed use benefit with benefiting councils comes through as well. Well, it's good for visitors is good for residents. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Um, and so yeah, the wording as it stands, we, we just don't, we would see it would be a, it, you would have to, you would have to contort yourself to make it work. Uh, and that I wouldn't think would be the intent of the, the legislation.

Thank you. Uh, la Uh, thank you. Uh, thanks for your comments and your submission. You commented on the government's ETH agenda, and the wording doesn't seem to reflect, uh, how that can be done in the tourism tourism industry. Can you just comment a bit further on that, please? Well, you know, if we've, um, the growth agenda is about doubling tourism, tourism excerpts by 20 30, 34.

The industry supports that we believe, you know, our New Zealand place and international markets, so that's absolutely doable, but we have to have the ability to invest on our supply side elements to make sure we've got the capacity, and we've got the quality, uh, to, to do, you know, to do really well by our visitors.

So, um, you know, regional airports might need to, uh, invest in their, their capacity or systems to grow a lot of those re local, local government owned. Um, the, you know, the, the, um, uh, to, to get that growth in tourism, you're going to need to have other strategies like seasonality and dispersal.

Um, both of those believers are reasonably weak, but local governments hold the really good ones, which is about organizing a program of events, uh, and the like. So, um, you know, it's gonna, it's going to, to be able to achieve the goal, it's gonna take a lot of people doing a lot of things in different sectors. Uh, and, and I, you know, we argue that local government is certainly gonna have an important role in that.

And just a quick sentence to share is around the wording. 'cause you did specifically comment on the wording of that. So yes, there's sectors, yes, there grow it, but the wording has to be reflected in this bill to achieve it. Um, I, I, I think that if the bill enables economic, um, development and including tourism, yes, um, that will give sufficient per guidance to councils that, that, that is actually one of their jobs, um, you know, under legislation.

So, so that's why we are seeking the, the placement of, you know, the economic development in the appropriate place. Thank you. Two, Two quick slightly different questions. Um, the first one is that the minister of tourism has expressed some concern that currently some councils are not contributing to their regional tourism organizations at all.

Um, do you think this bill will help those concerns in any way as it stands? Um, I, I would hope so. Um, you know, because at the moment the local council roles and tourism sort of, um, they're not, they're not defined in any legislation. Uh, they are, they are, uh, are done, uh, you know, as, as the council sees fit for the benefit of their communities.

Um, and, and, and, you know, and I'll use the example of, uh, the, the Waikato, uh, where there are seven local authorities contributing to one regional tourism organization. And, uh, that is always a, a very tricky exercise to get sufficient of those seven local authorities be contributing to, to, you know, providing enough funding for an effective regional tourism organization.

So making it clear that it is a role, I think would assist in that. Um, well, that would be with your amendment, not as it stands, Uh, not as it stands, not with, with argument. Thank you. Um, the other question I had, and it probably does relate to doubling the tourism numbers, but spreading both in time and space.

Um, a lot of our tourism is reliant on, um, healthy state of nature. You have put in suggested some changes to the purpose that look about economic growth, but, um, surely tourism also relies on a healthy and thriving natural environment for many, not all, but many of the activities.

Um, um, I, I agree with you. Um, uh, for tourism to flourish, uh, our communities have to flourish. Our environment has to flourish. Uh, we are working really hard to make tourism an agent good of nature in New Zealand. So we we're contributing to, uh, pest control and water quality, uh, and, and working with Department of Conservation and our concessionaries do a vast amount of work in those areas.

So we, we are really fostering that strength and relationship between tourism, uh, for good, for conservation and our nature in New Zealand. So as we grow, that will only provide more ability to do more. Good. I understand the good work that many operators are doing, and that the tourism industry overall is trying to make.

What I'm trying to focus on is the purpose of local government as expressed in the, in the draft bell doesn't really talk much about environmental health. Um, yes, I, I, I see, see the, the, uh, the point of difference, um, and in, in terms of the wording of the bill, I, I can't really comment on that.

Um, uh, but, but I know that a tourism industry would interpret a growing industry as one where it needs to contribute more, um, in terms of the role itself of local government, I'm sort of, uh, I'm, I haven't actually specifically looked into that. Thank You. Thank you very much for your time, and thanks for giving us a few extra minutes.

It was really very helpful. Thank you. Thank you so Much. Um, we're gonna move next to Citizens Advice Bureau online, and I think we're gonna be joined by Kerry Dalton, the chief executive. Just wait for, um, maybe her to turn her camera on now that she's promoted.
Welcome, Kerry. Thanks for joining us. We've got 10 minutes set aside for you. Um, I'll let you know when there's a minute to run, but otherwise, the time's all yours,

Kerry Dalton, I'm the Chief Executive of the Citizens of Ice Bureau, or the CAB as we are affectionately known. Um, the CAB is staffed by over 2000 fully trained volunteers from 80 CAB sites across roa. We provide free help to, to anyone to navigate their issues and problems and understand their options as a universal service.

There are no barriers to people seeking our help. The CAB is often a first port of call and is also where council and central government agencies send people when they don't have the answers or the time to help. Last year, c AABs helped over 300,000 clients to understand their rights and responsibilities and connect with services and support Through our service, we increase the engagement and participation of people in their communities by empowering them to know what their rights and options are, so that they're able to contribute

to a well-functioning civil society. This in turn, underpins and supports economic growth as our service supports people to be aware, knowledgeable, resourced, and skilled to participate in their community and contribute more broadly to society.

More than a hundred thousand people have volunteered countless hours to helping others in their community during our 55 years of existence. This has strengthened communities and democracy throughout al New Zealand. We consider it essential that the purpose statement of the acts puts community wellbeing at its center as local government makes decisions by and on behalf of communities.

The four wellbeings acknowledge that local government has a broader role in fostering strong and livable communities, rather than simply providing core services. Therefore, we oppose the introduction of the new purpose statement and the removal of the references to the four wellbeings from the act.

We acknowledge the government's desire to alleviate rates, pressures for communities. However, removing the four wellbeings from the purpose of local government is not the way to do this. As the bill's explanatory note acknowledges rates rises have been primarily driven by the rising costs of infrastructure, the rising costs of what this bill defines.

As core, the Department of Internal Affairs regulatory impact statement states that cost pressures on councils are being driven by capital and operating cost escalation flowing from supply chain upheaval and a tight labor market during the COVID-19 pandemic and accelerated headline inflation since it also states that infrastructure costs have long been a major cause of rate increases with councils needing to upgrade infrastructure, especially for water and wastewater treatment plants, and invest in more infrastructure to meet growth demands.

It also advised that proposed changes when considered in isolation are unlikely to benefit communities more than the status quo. Given this, we strongly argue for the retention of the four wellbeings as part of the core purpose of local government, because of how important they are.

The four wellbeings promote the sense of neighborhood and belonging that in turn promotes safety and security connection, helpfulness, community mindedness, weaving together stronger communities, which in turn provides the conditions for stronger economic growth with the growing occurrence of severe weather and flooding events, and the likelihood of a higher frequency of such events.

As climate change continues, we are seeing a heightened sense of the importance of having in place strong social support structures to help people navigate their way through the myriad of new and growing problems and challenges. These changes are bringing, having a focus on community wellbeing is a real investment in community resilience against the shocks of climate change and weather and other emergencies.

Councils cannot be reduced to just services and facilities. They have a broader and important role in building and maintaining real community strength, resilience and wellbeing. And references to the four wellbeings must remain in the act. In our submission, I have asked for the community's help in protecting the vital role of the CAB in communities.

Each CAB is its own legal entity, and councils are the primary funder of most of their local cas. While councils have long recognized the crucial role that the CAB plays in local communities, the repeal of the four wellbeings and the introduction of the new purpose statement in 2012 lead to some councils being uncertain as to whether they could continue to fund their local CAB at the time.

They would only enter into short-term funding agreements. Because of this lack of clarity, council funding is essential to the ongoing survival of most c AABs. If it is withdrawn, these CAS would no longer exist. Given the current bill is reducing the same, it is introducing the same amendments that caused uncertainty in our funding.

Before we ask that there is clarification provided in the bill that the CAB does come under the definition of a local public service, and that specific reference is made to the CAB as a service that councils can fund, not must fund, but can fund.

We also wish to make the point that our local S receive no central government funding at all. We consider that this is wrong and that central government should be contributing more, not as replacement funding for council funding, but to enable the growth and development and sustainability of such a vital service to meet the changing needs of people.

This is even more pertinent in a world where government services, both local and central, are relying on community partners like the CAB to fill the human gaps created by a digital first or digital only presence. Uh, and to support our role in helping people access and navigate services, including government services online, it is appropriate for local government to be funding c AABs, but central government also needs to do its bit, and the fact that it is not adequately doing so while relying on the CAB service so much

is an irony in relation to the drivers and stated rationale of this bill. We therefore ask that this committee recommend that there is an increase in central government funding for the essential service that the CAB provides. Thank you very much.

Um, here, pretty clearly it sounds like your main concern is around funding for Citizens Advice Bureau specifically is, is that correct? There's two aspects to this. One is about needing to retain the focus of local government on supporting community wellbeing.

And the other is in relation to the CAB, that it is made clear that if these amendments do proceed, that, um, the CAB is, um, able to be considered as a public service that councils can fund. Thank, thank you. I mean, I've met the local, um, CAB on the company coast.

They do a an incredible job. So I've seen firsthand the work they do. It's, it's your, your team there. Do a really good job. Um, I I I'm just wanting to make sure I understand. You've said that if, if this changes and therefore councils might not provide some funding that, that, that, that you would have to close.

I'm, I'm just wondering was, was that the experience back in say, 20 16, 17 18? In The end, um, we, um, we managed to continue to get the funding from the council. The minister of local government actually wrote a letter clarifying that, um, the CAB was able to be considered as a public service to provide that clarity.

Um, we are seeking that same clarity from this committee. Okay, I understand. So, so the wording of the bill, even at that time, was okay. It was just you needed the clarity. Uh, we needed the, the explicit, I think that what happened was that the councils were uncertain as to, because it was a signal about the need to reduce, um, or, um, yeah, reduce their, their, um, their services and their scope that they were unsure as to where the CAD sat in that.

And we just want the clarity that the CAB is a service that would meet the definition of public service. Thank you. And I appreciate the clarity. I'm, I'm not trying to, to have a go here, I just want to stand, 'cause I've read some of your older annual reports and they talk about that lack of funding clarity.

Like I'm looking at the 20 21 1 at the moment, and, and I can just see that your teams have been working really hard and, and amongst some uncertainty over many, many years, um, to deliver really great stuff. And I'm just wanting to under make sure I've heard you really clearly. Yes. And, uh, the other really clear, so one, there was a specific, there were specific situations where council officials were saying, we are not going to enter into a long-term agreement with you a three year one because we are not sure where we are going

to land in relation to these changes that are being brought in. But the other point I really wanna make is to get this committee's help in relation to our border front funding issue, which actually relates to central government funding as well. Um, as I said in my submission, that, um, central government needs to step up, um, in terms of recognizing and supporting the role that the CAB plays in helping people navigate central government services as well.

Tha thank you. And I think you've made that really, really, really well. And, and obviously we're aware of that in MB and DIA contracts and things. Um, unfortunately we're out of t time there. Did you want something really quick? Just a really quick stop on that. So CA bank helps people navigate both council and central go government issues.

What's the split in funding, if you happen to know it, between what the local CAS get from council and anything they might receive from central government? They currently get no funding from central government. That's what I thought. Thank you. Thanks so much. And we're gonna now move to Hamilton City Council, uh, who are also joining us online.

So they sought out problems course government. Good morning. Good morning. Welcome, uh, your worship and, and team there in, I'm not gonna mention City of the future, but it's, uh, great to see you. Uh, you've got 10 minutes for your submission and I'll let you know when there's a minute to run. Otherwise, it's all yours. Thank you very much. Definitely the city of the now as well.

Just put that in there. Uh, thank you for the opportunity to present. We really appreciate that. Um, it's been a bit of a compressed timescale, so we're in the odd situation of, uh, me being the mayor for approximately two more days potentially. And then the new council having to get their head around all the complexity of this, um, um, soon after reelection.

And just making the point that of course, that, um, some mayors, uh, are tied up with their electioneering, not me, but also, uh, some councils will consider, will see considerable turnover. So their ability to understand the detail will be, uh, under this compressed timescale will be a little bit difficult. Um, like everyone around the country, I'd love to be able to deliver low rates and make that promise and stick to it.

But, um, what we, uh, come up against really is the inability to raise any other sufficient revenue for the fast growth that a city like ours is experiencing. Um, we do not have many, uh, sources of funding to rely upon, and the majority of the funding that we do, the revenue that we do get in, is actually directed to core infrastructure.

When you consider, uh, water and transport being the two largest, uh, and then some critical community amenities, but basically core infrastructure, uh, we feel the, um, Bill's explanatory note does rightly acknowledge that rates are primarily increasing due to these critical infrastructure costs.

So you can see the link in our view. And when you look at some of the work that Trics and others are putting out, it's quite clear that the cost increases faith by local governments very, uh, very firmly within the construction labor markets that are, uh, I think Brad Olson has often suggested it might be as high as 27% if there was such a thing as a local government inflation index.

Uh, but most of our work is on the core services. So the potential for us to reduce rates will be limited outside of getting new funding tools. Uh, addressing the shortcomings of local government's funding and financing system therefore becomes critical before limiting council's ability to collect rates.

Um, because otherwise we are just going to have to put on hold or push out until the never, never critical elements of growth infrastructure for. And I can give you one example might be the Southern wastewater plant required to open up that whole area of land in the north white part in the South Hamilton and the airport precinct.

Um, the current rating system is just straining for metros like Hamilton and a fast growing. And I know I have similar conversations with my colleague mayor, um, Mahe over in the, in to, to, uh, we therefore we prefer, or we'd strongly recommend that the bill's implementation be deferred until July, 2027 to align with the long-term planning cycle.

We have, in fact, embedded in our long-term plan and just gone through an annual plan. Implementing the bill before then will force us to amend our LTP significantly to undergo, uh, rigid consultation requirements and, um, further audits, significant cost to council. And the other part I want to mention is the, um, clarity on core services.

Uh, we approve of, um, some of the current thinking, but we think it needs to go a little bit further and a little bit clearer. For example, waste management. We believe it needs to encompass not just the collection and disposal of waste, but look at the full scope of waste minimization.

And for example, we were one of the first councils to roll out food collections, waste food collections. Recreational facilities remains a little bit unclear to us. Do multipurpose venues like halls and community centers count in that definition. Um, cultural facilities such as art galleries for those included.

And, um, we support that. A better word to encompass these things would be community amenities as in the previous legislation. And I just note that in the case of Hamilton, uh, a number of those community recreational facilities and community amenities, um, underpin a strong tourism and therefore add to the, um, general economy.

We recommend the complete removal of section 11 A from the bill or amending as set out in the submission, uh, transparency and accountability amendments. Um, we support with additions outlined in the submission. Uh, so reporting on consultant and contractor expenditure, standardizing codes of conduct and standing orders, and ensuring elective members have access to necessary information.

We would caution that, um, councils must retain flexibility to reflect local needs over standardization, may reduce responsiveness and add unnecessary work and confidentiality. And responsible information handling must also be maintained. So in the regulatory relief and governance improvements, we support, um, with some suggestions, um, outlined in our submission, removing the mandatory six yearly service delivery reviews, extending the chief executive employment terms, um, from two to five years, clarifying the role

of acting chief executives in signing compliance certificates, recognizing third party funding and development contributions. So in summary, our key recommendations are remove clauses that redefine the purpose of local government and introduce the core services clause, retain the four wellbeings to ensure clarity, consistency, and community relevance.

Avoid rates capping based on narrow D definitions of core services, ensure changes, reduce, not increase compliant costs, maintain flexibility and governance and planning to reflect diverse community priorities. Retain the requirement to consider TE Maori and C-C-A-C-C-O appointments, support provisions that improve transparency, reduce red tape and strengthen accountability.

So in closing, um, thank you again for the opportunity to present and we welcome any further engagement and look forward to working with the committee to strengthen local government in a way that support supports communities, delivers values and respects the diverse roles council plays across New Zealand.

Here we go. Can you hear me? Yes. Yeah. Good. Yeah, co Stevens Koua. Um, yeah, so thanks for having me here to talk and, um, uh, KDA to, um, minister Watts. I'm looking forward to seeing you and Rota in a couple of weeks at the Residues revenues conference.

And I just wanted to go on record with, um, with some concerns, some rather deep concerns about this bill and the, the framing around it, which is the, um, stated purpose of less, uh, less interference and greater localism. But what it's actually doing is putting more constraints and more strictures on councils.

And I note in the principles, uh, at the, at the heading of the, of the, the bill are, uh, providing relief from cost of living pressures. And of course, uh, all of us who pay rates have seen this, uh, seen this very, uh, very pointedly. But, um, as a lot of us understand, this is partially a manufactured crisis because, uh, the single biggest cost that council space is in infrastructure.

So the redefinition of core services, uh, is, is a little bit facile because councils are already devoting the largest portion of their spend on investing in and maintaining assets. And those are, those are the, the roads and the pipes and, and the services or the, the things that deliver the services.

And, um, you know, we can, we can point to one large decision made by this government to repeal three waters, which took away, uh, a very large, um, assumption of a financial backstop that councils had that has been effectively yanked out from underneath them. And now they're faced with how to improve in, in what, in many cases is an infrastructure that's been run down for decades.

And there's, there's nowhere else for them to turn. So, so this is, uh, you know, this is sort of an untenable situation. A lot of councils are, are backed up against the wall with this. So there needs to be, um, a, a better system of funding, funding vehicles to deliver the investment and maintenance that these asset bases require. Uh, another concern is that this, the language in the bill, uh, is, is very, very direct about cost effectiveness.

And, um, it has sort of a balance sheet mentality which ignores the fact that public services are delivered for a, uh, for a intangible good. The, the, the, you know, the, the, the greater good of, of the residents and rate payers in a certain council catchment. And you can't always ascribe a dollar value to those, but if you take them away, you do see burdens and, and detrimental outcomes.

So I think, I think a lot of that language is, um, is unnecessarily constraining. And what it does too, is it sets up a framework by which councils will be, will be measured and come up with incentives or sometimes coercion to sell off assets or to hand over service delivery to private entities.

And every time this happens, every time we see, we can look overseas and we can look within our own borders. Every time a council hands over one of these core services to a third party, we lose a lot of transparency, we lose accountability. But most of all, we lose cost effectiveness because as soon as you give something to a private sector entity to deliver, they've gotta make a profit because they've got to pay not just the, the salaries and the cost of delivering the service, but now there's an expectation from their shareholders

of dividends and in increased equity. So it's axiomatic. As soon as you bring the private sector into this, you're going to get either higher costs or decrease levels of service. There's no way around it. So setting up an environment where this is seen as a desirable outcome is basically dooming the residents of, uh, of localities in New Zealand to situations like we're seeing in the UK where water services get privatized and pretty soon you've got boiled water notices right, and left and beaches that can't be swam out anymore.

Another thing we've got is this, uh, this idea that, uh, that the wellbeings are not actually part of core service delivery. I think a lot of, uh, of residents of cities and regions would, would beg to differ because what, what's, uh, what's very real to us out here is that the, uh, the wellbeing of communities is very much something that has to be handled locally.

It's not being, it's not something that we can get from central government agencies, and the councils as well are at the sharp end of climate adaptation. So when we get these extreme rainfall events, when we get the floods and the, and the, you know, the massive weather events, it's the councils that are cleaning up the mess and the councils that have to upgrade their infrastructure to cope with the burden.

So once again, this is all landing on the councils who then have to go to their rate payers to finance the improvements to deal with these things. They're not getting a whole lot of help. So, um, my main concern is that by removing the wellbeings, we're removing one of the mechanisms the councils have to build a little bit more resilience and to deliver strategic outcomes and not just talk about the roads and the pipes and the reserves.

So I've got a just sort of broad, broad based picture of some solutions that would include keeping the wellbeings, keeping the conga requirements for contractors and CCOs, but in, uh, in a much more, um, foundational sense, we need to rethink of how we, of how we move financing requirements and how we move the financing responsibilities and have central government more in partnership with councils instead of just standing over them with a stick.

And one of those would be population index grants to be tagged for capital and maintenance of infrastructure. This would be, uh, this would be a good way to, to ameliorate the, the loss of the three waters provisions we add. Then another one would be, sorry, we, We, we are just almost right on time, so if you want to wrap things.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. And thank you to the committee for the opportunity to sit on the bill. My name is Theo Vander Clut. I'm a final year law student at the University of Auckland. I've written on local government law, particularly comparative local government law, and I'm a local body candidate standing for the Weiss Matar local board with the city vision ticket. I suppose the, the, before I get into the substance of my submission, Mr.

Chair, um, I think the cardinal point that I want to draw the committee's attention to is, is really that local challenges are best solved by local decision makers. And the question that I would pose to this committee is with the current bill in front of the committee, are we creating a relationship or a power dynamic between central and local government where central government can impose its will on local government?

And as a ancillary question to that, I would say if that is the case, are we creating a system or upholding a system of local government, or are we simply treating local government like a crown entity or a state own enterprise that can be directed, uh, rather than, um, having the, the u the democratic quality of decision making being upheld?

Firstly, um, I believe that, uh, that the current system, particularly with the four four Wellbeings, um, which has been mentioned in previous submissions as a really flexible way of, uh, encouraging good local government decision making and governance, um, because it gives the, um, local authorities the ability to consider a wide range of measures such as the cultural, social, economic, and environmental wellbeing of their communities, um, beyond simply the rate payer metric that certain center, right, uh, people in local government would have you believe.

Secondly, I believe that the core services, um, aspect of this bill is really a political rubric, uh, designed by this current government to bully local authorities and, um, local councils into delivering a certain core set of services that centers property owning people at the center of local government.

And they already are, um, at the center of local government when it comes to understanding the types of people that get elected. The, the, a lot of the convenience of local government is angled towards people of a wealthier class. So I don't believe we should simply be measuring local government or service delivery according to that metric.

And um, thirdly, and finally, and this is where I probably touch into a little bit of the research that I've done in the local government space. Um, and I think it was in the regulatory impact statement that informed this bill, um, taking inspiration from the local government Act, the New South Wales Local Government Act 1993 is a exercise that I would really caution members against.

It's a very old piece of legislation. It's a piece of legislation that tends to favor, um, a lot of ministerial discretion, particularly when it comes to amalgamation and also relieving councils of their duties. And I think that considering that this, um, this course services bill will be linked to how councils are, uh, dismissed, or how, uh, statutory relief is applied, that we need to be legislating in a way that actually encourages, um, more, or sorry, legislating outwardly rather than legislating in a way that favors arbitrary, um, discretion.

Uh, so I think that that if, uh, parliament were going to be influenced by other pieces of leg legislation across the ditch. Uh, the Victorian local Government Act 2020 is a really good starting point. So is the Queensland Local Government Act 2009, particularly when it comes to defining, uh, service delivery and uh, also the link to, uh, the Crown intervention, uh, provisions because ultimately this core services bill it is, is, uh, or describes one function of local government, but it will be, um, linked with, um, other bills such

as the rates capping legislation that we will see. I think earlier it may have been passed already later in the year, but, um, I think we need to be far more progressive in how we think about this and also leave this type of decision making to the communities that elect local councils and local boards in.
